note: AutoKernel (arXiv:2603.21331v1, Jaber & Jaber, RightNow AI) is an autonomous GPU-kernel optimization framework: an LLM agent iteratively edits a single Triton/CUDA C++ kernel file, a fixed benchmark harness verifies correctness through five stages and then measures throughput, and a keep/revert rule (git commit if kept, git reset if regressed) drives a greedy hill-climbing loop over 300-400 experiments per overnight run. Both manuscript claims check out. C096 (CS/algorithms domain grouping) is ACCURATE -- AutoKernel is squarely a CS/algorithms application. C097 (loop closed via reliable empirical/benchmark validation + LLM-guided search) is ACCURATE for the AutoKernel-specific portion: the evaluation function is a correctness-gated performance benchmark (never validation loss) measured empirically via Triton do_bench + CUDA event timing, with a fixed-eval firewall ('the benchmark is never modified by the agent... prevents gaming the evaluation') that directly supports 'reliable/high-reliability'; the search is an iterative LLM-guided keep/revert loop (Algorithm 1), matching the 'LLM-guided search' branch of the manuscript's 'evolutionary or LLM-guided' disjunction. Note AutoKernel is deliberately NOT evolutionary/RL/multi-agent (that is a design choice, and 'evolutionary' applies to the co-cited AutoML-Zero/algorithm-discovery, so the disjunction is fine). Reverse nuances are all NON-defeating: (1) the manuscript's class-level 'residual bottleneck is interpretability' fits AutoKernel loosely at best -- AutoKernel's OWN stated limitations are the LLM code-generation ceiling and single-GPU/single-kernel scope, not interpretability, and its outputs are human-readable kernel source browsable via git (arguably MORE interpretable than evolved AutoML-Zero programs); (2) headline H100 speedups are authors' own measurements of STARTER kernels, and matmul is explicitly not solved (278 TFLOPS = 28% of peak, loses to cuBLAS); (3) the flashiest numbers (B200 vectorsum leaderboard win, FP4-beats-CUTLASS 1.63-2.15x) are COMMUNITY-reported, not authors' controlled runs. None of these undercut C097's loop-closure claim, which rests on the authors' controlled harness (all 34 configs pass, zero failures).

C096.evidence: Manuscript main.tex L176 (BG): '...and CS/algorithms~\citep{real2020automlzero,surina2025algodiscovery,jaber2026autokernel}.' AutoKernel is unambiguously a computer-science / algorithms application: title 'AutoKernel: Autonomous GPU Kernel Optimization via Iterative Agent-Driven Search' (p.1); it is 'an open-source framework that applies an autonomous agent loop to GPU kernel optimization for arbitrary PyTorch models' (Abstract, txt L18-19), arXiv listed as [cs.LG]. It optimizes low-level compute artifacts (Triton/CUDA C++ kernels) and is benchmarked on KernelBench, a standard GPU-kernel-generation benchmark. Grouping it with AutoML-Zero and algorithm-discovery under 'CS/algorithms' is a faithful domain classification.

C097.evidence: Manuscript main.tex L450 (CHAR): 'AutoML-Zero, algorithm discovery, and AutoKernel~\citep{jaber2026autokernel} close the loop via reliable benchmark/empirical validation and evolutionary or LLM-guided search. Validation is high-reliability and the loop is fully closed...'. AutoKernel-specific portion is directly supported on both axes. (A) RELIABLE EMPIRICAL/BENCHMARK VALIDATION (GPU performance measurement): 'A five-stage correctness harness, covering smoke tests, shape sweeps across 10+ configurations, numerical stability under adversarial inputs, determinism verification, and edge-case coverage, ensures that every candidate kernel is validated before any speedup is recorded' (Abstract, txt L21-24); throughput is measured empirically -- '30s for performance benchmarking via Triton's do_bench' (Sec 3.3, txt L344-345) and, in evaluation, 'CUDA event timing, 200 iterations per configuration, and trimmed mean (dropping the top and bottom 10%)' (Sec 7, txt L560-561). Reliability/firewall: 'Fixed evaluation, mutable code. The benchmark is never modified by the agent. This firewall prevents gaming the evaluation, a real risk when the candidate generator also controls the scorer' (Sec 10, txt L866-867); demonstrated reliability: 'All 34 configurations pass all five verification stages with zero failures' (Sec 7.1, txt L761-762). (B) LOOP CLOSED VIA LLM-GUIDED ITERATIVE SEARCH: 'AUTOKERNEL mechanizes this loop. An LLM agent modifies a single kernel file, a fixed benchmark harness verifies correctness and measures throughput, and the result determines whether the change persists' (Sec 1, txt L68-70); Algorithm 1 formalizes the keep/revert loop, keeping a candidate iff 'pass and t' > 1.01 * tbest' else 'git reset --hard HEAD~1' (txt L313-319), running 300-400 experiments per overnight run (Abstract/Sec 3.3). This is genuinely an LLM-guided search (the 'LLM-guided' branch of the manuscript's 'evolutionary or LLM-guided' disjunction); AutoKernel is deliberately NOT evolutionary/RL/multi-agent (Sec 2.3/Sec 10 'simplicity over sophistication'), but the disjunction is satisfied by the LLM-guided branch and 'evolutionary' covers the co-cited systems. The digest's bottom-line answer to Layer-2 was YES on both empirical validation and iterative agent-driven search (results/jaber2026autokernel.digest.md, 'Method' section). The 'fully closed / high-reliability' characterization is corroborated by the authors' own controlled harness (zero failures across 34 configs).
C097.fix: None required for the AutoKernel-specific claim. Optional (LOW, not a defect): the class-level clause 'the residual bottleneck is interpretability---the discovered artifacts work but resist human understanding' is a weaker fit for AutoKernel than for the co-cited AutoML-Zero/FunSearch-style systems, because AutoKernel emits human-readable Triton/CUDA source with a browsable git experiment history and never names interpretability as its bottleneck (its stated limitations are the LLM code-generation ceiling and single-GPU/single-kernel scope, Sec 11). If a reviewer probes the interpretability framing, lean on the evolved-program members of the group rather than AutoKernel. No change needed to the loop-closure claim itself.

reverse_findings.fact: AutoKernel's own stated limitations are the underlying LLM's code-generation ceiling ('Complex techniques like software pipelining, custom PTX emission, and multi-CTA cooperative strategies may exceed current agent abilities') and single-device scope ('The system currently optimizes individual kernels on a single GPU; distributed kernels and multi-device memory management are out of scope') -- NOT interpretability.
reverse_findings.mismatch: The manuscript's class-level clause 'the residual bottleneck is interpretability' is an editorial synthesis over the CS/algorithms group; it is not something AutoKernel claims for itself, and arguably fits AutoKernel poorly since its outputs are readable kernel source with git history. Does NOT undercut C097's core loop-closure claim (which is about reliable validation + closed search loop, both well supported). Severity LOW.

reverse_findings.fact: The headline H100 speedups (RMSNorm 5.29x over eager / 2.83x over torch.compile, softmax 2.82x, cross-entropy 2.21x) are the authors' own measurements of the STARTER kernels at the largest tested size (Table 4); Sec 7.3 describes the optimization-loop dynamics separately but reports no post-loop end-state speedup for these kernels. Matmul is explicitly NOT solved: Triton starter reaches only 278 TFLOPS = 28% of H100's 989.5 TFLOPS peak and loses to cuBLAS/eager (0.29-0.43x); 'Matmul remains hard'.
reverse_findings.loc: Abstract txt L28-32; Sec 7.1 txt L757-760; Table 4 txt L595-620
reverse_findings.mismatch: No conflict with C097, which does not assert AutoKernel beats all baselines -- only that the loop is closed via reliable validation. This is a nuance on the DEGREE of optimization success, not on loop closure. Flagged so the manuscript does not over-read AutoKernel's speedups elsewhere. Severity INFO.

reverse_findings.fact: The most striking results are COMMUNITY-reported, not the authors' controlled experiments: 1st place on the vectorsum_v2 B200 leaderboard (44.086us) and a single-prompt Triton FP4 matmul 'reported to outperform CUTLASS by 1.63 to 2.15x' -- Table 5 is explicitly 'Community-reported' from 'a single agent prompt (~3 minutes)'.
reverse_findings.loc: Sec 7.2 txt L767-778; Table 5 caption txt L781-782
reverse_findings.mismatch: Does not affect C097's loop-closure claim, which rests on the authors' fixed harness (all 34 configs pass, zero failures), not on the community anecdotes. Relevant only if the manuscript were to cite the B200/FP4 numbers as validated results. Severity INFO.

reverse_findings.fact: Corroborating (supports C097): the 'reliable/high-reliability' descriptor is directly grounded in the design -- 'Fixed evaluation, mutable code. The benchmark is never modified by the agent. This firewall prevents gaming the evaluation' (Sec 10) and 'All 34 configurations pass all five verification stages with zero failures' (Sec 7.1). The evaluation function is a correctness-gated benchmark, not validation loss.
reverse_findings.loc: Sec 10 txt L866-867; Sec 7.1 txt L761-762; Sec 1 txt L73-74
reverse_findings.mismatch: None -- reinforces the manuscript. Severity INFO.
